critique.summary: The article advocates for integrating documentation directly into code repositories, emphasizing the benefits of version control, ease of updates, and the role of AI in maintaining documentation accuracy. It argues that this practice enhances collaboration and ensures that documentation evolves alongside the code.
mainPoints: Documentation should live alongside code for better version control.
AI agents can help maintain documentation accuracy and alignment with code.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made in the article about the benefits of keeping documentation in the same repository as code are generally accurate and reflect current trends in software development. The use of AI to assist in documentation maintenance is also supported by emerging practices in the industry, although the effectiveness can vary based on implementation.
considerations: The article does not address potential challenges in migrating existing documentation to repositories.
It overlooks the need for training and adaptation for teams unfamiliar with this approach.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the importance of documentation in software development, particularly in relation to AI tools. Many commenters emphasize that good documentation practices predate AI advancements and should be maintained for human benefit, while others explore the integration of AI in enhancing documentation processes.

comments.author: susam
comments.text: It is a bit weird to see LLMs suddenly being presented as the reason to follow what are basically long standing best practices...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a historical perspective on documentation practices, emphasizing their intrinsic value beyond AI influence.

comments.author: jmward01
comments.text: When I start a new project with a team I start off with asking 'how we will work' and part of that is 'how we will communicate'...
comments.analysis: The comment highlights the evolving nature of software engineering and the importance of documentation in team communication.

comments.author: themanmaran
comments.text: We just did this the other week and it's such a great setup using AI. Monorepos in general are better for coding agents since it's a single locati...
comments.analysis: This comment shares a practical experience of integrating AI with documentation, showcasing its benefits in a specific context.

comments.author: prepend
comments.text: That time was like 10 years ago. I think it’s been best practice to have docs in the repo for a long time...
comments.analysis: While the comment correctly points out the longevity of documentation practices, it lacks depth in analysis.

comments.author: theletterf
comments.text: There’s an irresistible, almost demoralizing irony in the fact that developers are discovering docs and accessibility only now due to AI...
comments.analysis: This comment presents a critical view on the delayed recognition of documentation's importance, though it leans towards emotional rhetoric.

comments.author: gbro3n
comments.text: Bit of a plug I suppose, but this was what motivated me to set up AS Notes, my VS code extension which makes VS Code a personal knowledge managemen...
comments.analysis: The comment serves as a self-promotion rather than a focused discussion on the topic, reducing its relevance.

comments.author: redgridtactical
comments.text: The biggest win for me with docs-in-repo isn't the AI angle, it's that pull requests can't land without updating the relevant docs...
comments.analysis: This comment effectively discusses the practical advantages of documentation in repositories, though it could elaborate further.

comments.author: petcat
comments.text: Out-of-band docs have always been a constant source of frustration and discrepancies. It's really difficult to keep readme.com docs updated with actual...
comments.analysis: The comment addresses common frustrations with documentation but lacks actionable insights.

comments.author: odie5533
comments.text: What about a OneDrive folder shared with all developers, mounted in a place the AI can access? Putting docs in git makes it slow to iterate and share...
comments.analysis: While proposing an alternative, the suggestion may overlook the advantages of version control in documentation.

comments.author: alansaber
comments.text: Not sure I agree with this. MD files need to be constantly synced to code state- why not just grep the code files? This is just more unstructured indexing
comments.analysis: This comment dismisses the value of documentation without providing a strong rationale, making it less constructive.

comments.author: whatever1
comments.text: More importantly move your docs from anything else to pure markdown. Finally we are free from weird file formats and superfluous syntax for docs.
comments.analysis: The comment lacks depth and relevance to the main discussion, focusing instead on a narrow aspect of documentation.

comments.author: jmclnx
comments.text: Sounds like they are saying use a repo like git for your documents to help AI read'understand' your docs. I am all for using a source control system...
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid point about using version control for documents but introduces unnecessary concerns about AI access.
